children.name: Numida
children.description: The best-known guineafowl genus, domesticated for millennia and widely introduced beyond its native range. Characterised by the bare crowned head and the characteristic spotted or pearl-grey plumage that gives the domesticated forms their familiar look.

children.name: Numida meleagris
children.description: The most widespread and familiar guineafowl, native to Africa south of the Sahara but domesticated worldwide. The bony casque atop the head gives the 'helmeted' name, and large flocks roam savanna and scrub giving loud, grating calls.

children.name: Guttera
children.description: Distinguished from other guineafowl by a prominent black crest of curly feathers on the crown. The two species occupy forest and woodland habitats in sub-Saharan Africa, with subtle plumage differences distinguishing the crested and plumed forms.

children.name: Guttera pucherani
children.description: A forest-dwelling guineafowl with a distinctive curly black crest and blue facial skin. Found from Senegal east through the Congo Basin to Kenya, preferring dense lowland forest edges and thickets.

children.name: Guttera plumifera
children.description: Endemic to the lowland rainforests of Central Africa, with a stiff, upright crest of black plumes rather than the tightly curled crest of its congener. Often difficult to observe as it skulks in dense undergrowth.

children.name: Acryllium
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the striking vulturine guineafowl, the largest and most colourful member of the family. The bare-skinned face and elongated neck feathers give it a distinctly vulturine appearance, and it inhabits dry savanna in East Africa.

children.name: Acryllium vulturinum
children.description: The largest guineafowl, easily recognised by its bare blue face and neck, chestnut eye patch, and elegant black-and-white scalloped plumage with long hackles. Inhabits arid savanna and thornbush of East Africa, where flocks run swiftly on long legs.

children.name: Agelastes
children.description: A small genus of two guineafowl species that lack the typical spotted plumage of the family, instead having unpatterned black or grey-brown feathers. Both are forest-dwelling and more secretive than other guineafowl.

children.name: Agelastes meleagrides
children.description: A striking black guineafowl with a snowy white breast and throat, inhabiting lowland rainforests of West Africa. Shy and declining due to deforestation and hunting, it is classified as Near Threatened.

children.name: Agelastes niger
children.description: Almost entirely black with a bare pinkish head and a white patch on the nape. Found in the dense lowland forests of the Congo Basin and Cameroon, foraging for seeds and invertebrates in loose family groups.
